entities.description: A parchment codex of i + 196 + i leaves, approximately 7.75 x 5.25 inches, written in several clear hands in one column. The target poem is in another hand from the preceding texts, begins on f. 186r, ends on f. 196r, and leaves f. 196v blank. James and Pink date the codex late fourteenth century; Patterson allows early fifteenth or possibly late fourteenth.

holding.notes: The Apollo Pink and James datasets are official repository publications of historical unpublished descriptions. Cambridge's current search guidance says many medieval manuscripts still rely on printed catalogues and directs questions to Special Collections.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Dd.11.89

physicalDescription.format: Codex; single column; James reports 32 lines per page, Pink 26-34
notes: James describes three or four clear, rather rough hands; the target is introduced as 'Another hand.'
Pink records several late-fourteenth-century hands, red headings, red and blue paraphs and initials.
Rebound by Douglas Cockerell & Son, Letchworth, in 1963.
Patterson's f. 186a-196a notation is normalized here to ff. 186r-196r; James says 196v is blank.

next_steps: Obtain complete page images of CUL Dd.11.89 and compare every span against ff. 186r-196r.
Classify abbreviation expansions, supplied letters, uncertain readings, and any other editorial functions separately.
Retain the edition's italic signal without silently normalizing it until direct evidence resolves each span.
openQuestions.question: What exact Cambridge manuscript features do Patterson's 77 italic spans inside the medieval poem represent?

next_steps: Submit a whole-codex request through Cambridge Imaging Services and identify ff. 186r-196r separately.
Ask whether existing internal photography or microfilm can reduce cost and whether covers, endleaves, blanks, every leaf, and edges can be included.
Obtain separate written terms for private research, repository redistribution, scholarly illustration, and commercial or print-on-demand use before publishing any supplied image.
notes: No complete public copy was found in the checked scope; that does not mean the manuscript has never been digitized.

next_steps: Ask Bodleian Special Collections about complete existing photography, microfilm, or a whole-codex imaging order and exact reuse terms.
Map ff. 130r-138r within any complete supplied object; do not substitute the public f. 139r slide, which begins after the target.
Perform or locate target-hand linguistic analysis rather than applying the Warwickshire and Oxfordshire labels for the preceding Prick of Conscience hands.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete facsimile of MS. Douce 141 be obtained, and what hand and dialect copied the IMEV 958 text on ff. 130r-138r?

next_steps: Consult work-specific scholarship and collate both witnesses.
Keep Cambridge manuscript dating, DIMEV's codex-level East Anglia label, Douce production, and abstract-work composition as separate claims.
Reassess whether James/Pink's late-fourteenth-century dating or Patterson's broader alternative is favored by current codicology.
openQuestions.question: Can the poem's composition date and place be established more narrowly than by the late-fourteenth- or early-fifteenth-century Cambridge attestation in England?

relations.subjectLabel: Digital Bodleian two-slide object for MS. Douce 141

reviewNotes: The primary transmission path is repository XML to U-M encoding to Patterson's 1916 edition to CUL Dd.11.89, ff. 186r-196r.
Bodleian MS. Douce 141, ff. 130r-138r, is the only other DIMEV witness and is collateral. No relation says Patterson transcribed or collated it.
Patterson's f. 186a-196a notation is normalized to ff. 186r-196r because James explicitly places the ending on 196a and says 196v is blank.

The encoded item contains the complete article, including one modern prose introduction and Patterson's signed closer. Those paratexts are retained in source accounting but do not make the medieval work prose, mixed-form, modern English, or Patterson-authored.

The XML contains 596 medieval verse L elements plus a terminal Amen closer. Patterson prints marginal line number 90 twice; all later displayed numbers are five behind XML position, so displayed 590 accompanies XML line 595 and the final verse line is unnumbered.

Seventy-seven HI elements occur in the poem and one in Patterson's affiliation. The likely expansion function of poem italics remains unverified without manuscript images and is recorded at low confidence.

No complete public facsimile of CUL Dd.11.89 or MS. Douce 141 was found in the dated search scope. Cambridge offers a current paid online imaging route; the Bodleian catalog provides a Special Collections contact. Neither negative search is an assertion of nondigitization.

The complete public two-slide Digital Bodleian object is cached, but its only images are f. 1r and f. 139r, so it has no target-work portion. Complete editions, OCR, catalogues, and versioned data releases are cached whole under the ignored source-cache/CME00111 directory.

Rights remain layer-specific: the medieval work and 1916 article are public domain in the United States; U-M applies NoC-US; Cambridge description datasets are CC BY 4.0; DIMEV is CC BY 4.0; MWM is CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; the two Bodleian slides are CC BY-NC 4.0; future supplied manuscript images require their exact order terms.

lineage.summary: The checked-in CME00111 XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00111, which encodes Frank A. Patterson's complete 1916 article 'A Sermon on the Lord's Prayer' from The Journal of English and Germanic Philology 15, pp. 406-418. Patterson directly transcribed Cambridge University Library MS Dd.11.89, ff. 186r-196r (his f. 186a-196a), adding one introductory paragraph, two notes, and a signed closer. The encoded medieval poem has 596 verse-line elements in five groups plus a terminal Amen closer; Patterson accidentally prints marginal line number 90 twice, so later displayed numbers are five below XML position. DIMEV 1573 / IMEV 958 identifies Bodleian MS. Douce 141, ff. 130r-138r, as the only other indexed witness; it is collateral, not a source of Patterson's transcription. Complete public edition, OCR, metadata, catalogue, and data-release objects are cached where available. No complete public facsimile of either manuscript was found in the dated search scope: Cambridge supplies a current whole-object imaging request route, while the only public Douce image object has two slides, f. 1r and f. 139r, and does not include the work.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: By the late fourteenth or early fifteenth century; exact composition date unresolved; first known publication 1916
catalogSummary.region: England; exact composition locality unknown. DIMEV indexes the Cambridge codex to East Anglia, but that witness-level localization is not treated as a composition place
catalogSummary.title: Exposition of the Pater Noster

catalogingSubject.label: Exposition of the Pater Noster
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous Middle English verse exposition of the Lord's Prayer indexed as DIMEV 1573 / IMEV 958, represented here by Frank A. Patterson's 1916 transcription of Cambridge University Library MS Dd.11.89, ff. 186r-196r. Patterson's article title, introductory paragraph, notes, signature, and affiliation are separately accounted source paratext, not medieval authorship, language, prose form, genre, or subject facets. Bodleian Library MS. Douce 141, ff. 130r-138r, is a collateral witness, not a source of Patterson's text or the U-M encoding.

contentParts.label: Patterson's article title and introduction

locators.value: DIV1/HEAD and direct P before LG[1]

notes: Modern English editorial paratext is not included in work-level language, prose-form, genre, subject, or authorship discovery fields.
contentParts.summary: The modern article heading and one prose paragraph identifying the manuscript, proposing a late-fourteenth- or early-fifteenth-century date, describing the poem's didactic interest, and citing the older Cambridge catalogue.

contentParts.label: Exposition of the Pater Noster

locators.value: LG[1]-LG[5], 596 L elements, and first CLOSER

notes: The separate first CLOSER contains the medieval terminal Amen and belongs to the work even though it is not an L element.
The 596 count describes the represented XML/transcription, not every witness or an abstract-work critical lineation.
contentParts.summary: The anonymous Middle English couplet exposition represented by the Cambridge witness, from the invocation to the communal final Amen.

contentParts.label: Patterson signature and affiliation
notes: The italic Columbia University affiliation accounts for the one HI element outside the medieval verse.
contentParts.summary: The modern signed closer naming Frank A. Patterson and Columbia University after the poem.

assertion.notes: This is a terminus from the represented witness, not an equation of manuscript production with composition.

dateStatements.display: Before or by the represented late-fourteenth- or early-fifteenth-century Cambridge witness; exact composition date unknown

assertion.notes: The range preserves the inspected sources' dating difference rather than selecting one silently.

dateStatements.display: Late fourteenth century according to James and Pink; Patterson allows the early fifteenth century

assertion.notes: Patterson said that he believed the poem had never been published; DIMEV v1.0 lists his edition for the represented witness.

dateStatements.display: 1916 (first known publication, by Frank A. Patterson)

extent.description: Whole encoded article accounting: one DIV1 and one HEAD; one modern introductory P; five medieval LG groups containing 596 L elements; two NOTE elements; 140 MILESTONE elements; thirteen PB elements marking printed pp. 406-418; two CLOSER elements (the medieval terminal Amen and Patterson's signed closer); one SIGNED element; one LB; and 78 HI elements. Seventy-seven HI elements occur inside medieval verse lines and one marks Patterson's Columbia University affiliation. Patterson's printed marginal line number 90 occurs twice, at XML verse lines 90 and 95; later printed numbers are five lower than XML position, so printed 590 marks XML line 595 and the final verse is XML line 596.

formStatements.description: A Middle English poem in aa rhyming couplets. The represented Cambridge transcription has 596 L elements in five XML verse groups, followed by a separately encoded terminal Amen; Patterson's prose introduction and signature remain separate source paratext.

assertion.notes: Patterson titled the edition a sermon and describes clerical inculcation of virtue; DIMEV more neutrally calls it an exposition.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The one encoded Latin marginal petition label and modern English article paratext are source-layer features and do not make the abstract medieval work bilingual.
The work is anonymous; Patterson is editor and transcriber, not author.
Patterson's a/b folio notation maps to modern recto/verso: f. 186a-f. 196a is recorded as ff. 186r-196r. James explicitly says the poem ends on 196a and 196v is blank.
The two surviving witnesses identified by DIMEV are Cambridge Dd.11.89, ff. 186r-196r, and Bodleian Douce 141, ff. 130r-138r. Patterson used the Cambridge witness only; Douce is collateral.

Printed marginal line number 90 is duplicated. Consequently the XML's actual 596 verse lines must not be reported as Patterson line 596: his last displayed number is 590 beside XML line 595, followed by one unnumbered verse line and the terminal Amen closer.
The one Latin marginal petition label and Patterson's modern English frame are retained as source-layer details but are excluded from abstract-work language and prose-form discovery.

The complete JEGP volume, OCR, Internet Archive metadata, DIMEV v1.0 release, both Cambridge description datasets, the complete MWM data release, the current Bodleian catalogue TEI, and the complete published two-slide Bodleian object are cached under source-cache/CME00111. The cache remains Git-ignored.

next_steps: Consult modern work-specific scholarship beyond the two-witness DIMEV record and historical descriptions.
Distinguish composition language from each target hand before adding a narrower region facet.
Do not transfer DIMEV's codex-level East Anglia label or the analyzed Douce ff. 1r-129v hands to this work without target-specific evidence.
openQuestions.question: When and where was the poem composed, and what work-specific dialect evidence can be established for each surviving witness?

next_steps: Obtain complete Cambridge page images and compare every italic span against ff. 186r-196r.
Separate abbreviation expansions, supplied letters, uncertain readings, and any other functions at segment level.
Preserve the edition's italic signal until Patterson's undocumented convention can be verified directly.
openQuestions.question: What exact manuscript features do Patterson's 77 italic spans inside the poem represent?

next_steps: Ask Cambridge Imaging Services for a whole-codex quote and exact reuse terms, then map ff. 186r-196r within the supplied object.
Ask Bodleian Special Collections whether complete photography or an existing microfilm/digital surrogate can be supplied, then map ff. 130r-138r.
Record any supplied whole-object files in the ignored source cache without inferring public redistribution rights from private research access.
notes: No complete public copy was found in the dated search scope; this does not establish that either manuscript has never been digitized.
openQuestions.question: Can complete, reusable color facsimiles of CUL Dd.11.89 and Bodleian MS. Douce 141 be obtained and mapped at work level?

assertion.notes: DIMEV's East Anglia entry describes CUL Dd.11.89 at manuscript-index level; it is not promoted to a composition-place claim. The two analyzed Douce Prick of Conscience hands end before this work, and MWM says the other texts have not been linguistically analyzed.

notes: Edition-level responsibility, not medieval authorship.

subjects.label: The Lord's Prayer and its seven petitions
Sin, forgiveness, temptation, and moral instruction
The seven deadly sins and Last Judgment

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English exposition of the Lord's Prayer in rhyming couplets. After presenting and interpreting the prayer's seven petitions, the poem develops instruction on divine fatherhood, Christian conduct, sin and forgiveness, temptation, the seven deadly sins, the Trinity, Christ's Passion, the Eucharist, judgment, and salvation. CME00111 represents Patterson's 1916 transcription of the Cambridge witness; his short modern introduction and signed article frame are present in the source item but are not part of the medieval work.

titles.supplied_by: General Corpus from DIMEV and the Medieval West Midlands catalogue

notes: This is a modern editorial title and does not establish medieval self-identification as a sermon.
